title: Coleus scaber
taxonomy: Coleus scaber belongs to the family Lamiaceae, commonly known as the mint family. It is a species of flowering plant in the genus Coleus, which is a group of plants native to tropical regions. The genus Coleus is further divided into several species, with Coleus scaber being one of them. This species is native to tropical Africa and is characterized by its small, pink or purple flowers and oval-shaped leaves. The exact classification of Coleus scaber may vary depending on the source, but it is generally considered to be a member of the Lamiaceae family.
uses: Coleus scaber is known to be used in traditional medicine, particularly in India and other parts of Asia. The plant is believed to possess anti-inflammatory properties, which may help in reducing pain and swelling. intro: Coleus scaber, also known as Malay Coleus or Coleus barbatus, is a perennial plant species native to Southeast Asia, characterized by its vibrant purple flowers and attractive foliage. This plant has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which can aid in relieving pain, reducing fever, and promoting wound healing. The bioactive constituents of Coleus scaber include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and iridoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Coleus scaber is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and it is also used in the production of herbal teas, capsules, and topicals, such as creams and ointments.
